content:
# Acts Chapter 25 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 256-257 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_025_page_001.jpg, ACT_025_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Three days after arriving in the province, Festus traveled from Caesarea up to Jerusalem.

**2** There the chief priests and the leading men among the Jews brought their charges against Paul before him, pressing him urgently

**3** and asking as a personal favor that he have Paul transferred to Jerusalem — all the while plotting to ambush and kill him along the way.

**4** But Festus replied that Paul was being held in Caesarea and that he himself would be returning there shortly.

**5** "Let your leading men come down with me," he told them, "and if this man has done anything wrong, they can press charges against him there."

**6** After spending no more than eight or ten days among them, Festus went down to Caesarea. The next day he took his seat on the judge's bench and ordered Paul brought before him.

**7** When Paul appeared, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem surrounded him, hurling one serious accusation after another — none of which they could prove.

**8** Paul made his defense: "I have committed no offense whatsoever against the Jewish law, or against the temple, or against Caesar."

**9** But Festus, wanting to do the Jews a political favor, turned to Paul and asked, "Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and stand trial before me on these charges there?"

**10** Paul answered, "I am standing right now before Caesar's tribunal — which is exactly where I ought to be tried. I have done nothing wrong against the Jews, as you yourself know perfectly well.

**11** If I am guilty — if I have done anything that deserves death — I do not refuse to die. But if none of their accusations hold up, no one has the right to hand me over to them as a favor. I appeal to Caesar!"

**12** After conferring with his advisory council, Festus declared: "You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you will go."

**13** Some days later, King Agrippa and Bernice arrived in Caesarea to pay their respects to Festus.

**14** Since they were staying for several days, Festus laid Paul's case before the king. "There is a man here," he said, "whom Felix left behind as a prisoner.

**15** When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the Jewish elders brought charges against him and demanded a guilty verdict.

**16** I told them that it is not Roman practice to hand over any accused person before he has faced his accusers in person and been given a fair chance to defend himself against the charges.

**17** So when they came here with me, I wasted no time. The very next day I took my seat on the bench and ordered the man brought in.

**18** But when his accusers stood up, they did not charge him with any of the crimes I had been expecting.

**19** Instead, they had disputes with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus who had died — but whom Paul kept insisting was alive.

**20** I was at a loss how to investigate such matters, so I asked whether he would be willing to go to Jerusalem and be tried there on these issues.

**21** But Paul appealed to have his case held over for the decision of His Majesty the Emperor. So I ordered him kept in custody until I could send him on to Caesar."

**22** Agrippa said to Festus, "I would like to hear this man myself." "Tomorrow," Festus replied, "you will hear him."

**23** The next day Agrippa and Bernice came with great pageantry and entered the audience hall, accompanied by the military commanders and the city's leading men. At Festus's command, Paul was brought in.

**24** Festus said, "King Agrippa and all who are present with us: you see this man. The entire Jewish community — both in Jerusalem and here — has petitioned me about him, shouting that he should no longer be allowed to live.

**25** But I found that he had done nothing deserving death. And since he himself appealed to the Emperor, I decided to send him.

**26** Yet I have nothing definitive to write to His Majesty about him. That is why I have brought him before all of you — and especially before you, King Agrippa — so that after this examination I may have something to put in writing.

**27** For it seems unreasonable to me to send a prisoner to Rome without specifying the charges against him."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 3 — "plotting to ambush and kill him along the way":** The Greek (ενεδραν ποιουντες) uses a participle construction that reveals the request was itself the trap. The Sinaiticus text makes clear the transfer request and the murder plot were simultaneous — the favor they asked *was* the ambush. I rendered this to preserve that sinister simultaneity.

**Verse 5 — "if this man has done anything wrong":** Festus's language here (ατοπον, "out of place") is diplomatically vague. He is not yet convinced of guilt and uses a conditional that subtly signals skepticism. The dynamic rendering preserves his measured, bureaucratic tone.

**Verse 9 — "wanting to do the Jews a political favor":** The Greek (θελων χαριν καταθεσθαι) carries the weight of political currency — this is not personal kindness but calculated diplomacy. Adding "political" makes explicit what the original audience would have understood from context.

**Verse 10 — "which is exactly where I ought to be tried":** Paul's response (ου με δει κρινεσθαι) carries legal force — he is asserting his Roman citizenship rights. The emphatic "exactly" captures the force of his insistence that Roman jurisdiction is not optional but obligatory.

**Verse 11 — "no one has the right to hand me over to them as a favor":** The verb (χαρισασθαι) echoes the same root as Festus's political favor-giving in verse 9. Paul throws the word back in Festus's face — "you cannot *gift* me to them." The dynamic rendering makes this confrontation explicit.

**Verse 19 — "a certain Jesus who had died — but whom Paul kept insisting was alive":** Festus's account to Agrippa is fascinating in its outsider perspective. The Sinaiticus reading (τινος Ιησου τεθνηκοτος ον εφασκεν ο Παυλος ζην) reduces the resurrection to a baffling claim by one man. The dash and the word "kept insisting" capture Festus's bewildered tone — this is Christianity as seen through Roman bureaucratic eyes.

**Verse 21 — "His Majesty the Emperor":** The Greek (του Σεβαστου) is the formal title "Augustus/Sebastos," the divine honorific of the emperor. "His Majesty the Emperor" captures the formal weight of the title in modern English better than a transliteration would.

**Verse 23 — "with great pageantry":** The Greek (μετα πολλης φαντασιας) literally yields "with much phantasia" — display, spectacle, pomp. "Pageantry" captures the theatrical, almost performative quality of the royal entrance, which Luke deliberately contrasts with Paul the prisoner.

**Verse 27 — "it seems unreasonable to me":** The word (αλογον) means literally "without reason/logic." Festus's closing admission is remarkable — he has a prisoner he must send to Rome but cannot articulate what crime the man committed. This is Luke's narrative irony at its sharpest: the entire Roman legal apparatus has examined Paul and found nothing to write down.

— PNEUMA
